heroOverview: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma; ICD-10: M34) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by progressive fibrosis of the skin and internal organs, vasculopathy, and immune dysregulation. It affects approximately 20-50 per 100,000 people in Western populations, with a female-to-male ratio of 4:1 and peak onset between ages 35-65. Raynaud phenomenon is nearly universal, typically appearing years before skin or organ changes. The disease is divided into limited cutaneous and diffuse cutaneous subtypes based on skin distribution. Pulmonary complications — interstitial lung disease and pulmonary arterial hypertension — account for over 60% of disease-related deaths. Although no therapy halts fibrosis globally, targeted treatment of specific organ complications substantially improves survival and quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: 22 per 100,000 in the United States (Mayes et al., Arthritis Rheum 2003); higher in sub-Saharan African populations
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio 4:1; Black women have 3-fold higher incidence and more severe disease than White women
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset ages 35-65; rare before age 20; incidence peaks in the fourth to sixth decade
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 2.5 million people worldwide live with systemic sclerosis (Global Burden of Disease 2021)

definition: Systemic sclerosis (SSc; ICD-10: M34) is a systemic connective tissue disease driven by the triad of immune activation (autoantibody production, T-cell infiltration), vasculopathy (endothelial injury, Raynaud phenomenon, digital ulcers), and fibrosis (excessive extracellular matrix deposition by activated myofibroblasts in skin, lung, heart, kidney, and gastrointestinal tract). The 2013 ACR/EULAR classification criteria require a weighted score including: skin thickening proximal to the metacarpophalangeal joints (sufficient alone for classification), Raynaud phenomenon, SSc-related autoantibodies, and nailfold capillaroscopy findings. Two major subtypes are defined by skin involvement. In limited cutaneous SSc (lcSSc, formerly CREST syndrome), skin thickening is restricted to the hands, forearms, feet, and face; this subtype is associated with anticentromere antibody and carries a relatively preserved 10-year survival but significant risk of late pulmonary arterial hypertension. In diffuse cutaneous SSc (dcSSc), rapid skin thickening extends to the proximal limbs and trunk within 5 years of onset; anti-topoisomerase I (Scl-70) antibody predominates, and risk of early interstitial lung disease, scleroderma renal crisis, and myocardial fibrosis is substantially higher. SSc is managed by a multidisciplinary team led by a rheumatologist with specialist input from pulmonology, cardiology, gastroenterology, and nephrology.

typesClassification.description: Skin thickening confined to hands, forearms, feet, lower legs, and face. Previously called CREST syndrome (Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia). Anticentromere antibody positive in 60-70%. PAH develops late (after 15-20 years) in 10-20% of patients.

Rapidly progressive skin thickening extending to proximal limbs and trunk within 5 years of first non-Raynaud symptom. Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) positive in 25-35%. Higher risk of early ILD, scleroderma renal crisis, and cardiac fibrosis.
Internal organ involvement with SSc-specific autoantibodies and Raynaud phenomenon but without significant skin thickening. Rare; accounts for 2-5% of SSc.
SSc features combined with another CTD (SLE, polymyositis, Sjogren syndrome); associated with anti-U1 RNP or anti-PM/Scl antibodies. Prognosis depends on the dominant CTD features.

primarySymptoms: Raynaud phenomenon with triphasic color change (white-blue-red) of fingers on cold exposure or stress, present in over 95% of patients and typically the first symptom years before skin changes.
Progressive skin thickening and hardening (sclerodactyly) starting at the fingers, causing reduced grip strength, digital skin pitting scars, and finger tip ulcers.
Digital ulcers on fingertips and knuckles from ischemia, present in 30-50% of SSc patients and a marker of more severe vasculopathy.
Dysphagia and gastroesophageal reflux from esophageal dysmotility, affecting over 75% of patients; the smooth muscle of the lower esophagus is progressively replaced by fibrous tissue.
Exertional dyspnea and dry cough from interstitial lung disease, the most common serious organ complication occurring in 35-75% depending on subtype and antibody.
Facial skin tightening, reduced oral aperture, beaked nose, and loss of facial expression from fibrosis of the face and perioral tissues.
Calcinosis cutis — visible and palpable calcium deposits under the skin of fingers, forearms, and elbows — more common in lcSSc than dcSSc.
Telangiectasia (multiple small dilated capillaries visible on skin and mucous membranes) as mat-like spots on face, hands, and lips in lcSSc.
Musculoskeletal pain, tendon friction rubs (a leathery creaking felt over tendons), and flexion contractures from skin and tendon fibrosis.
Severe fatigue in 60-80% of patients, partly from inflammatory cytokines, partly from deconditioning, and partly from disrupted sleep from Raynaud attacks and GERD.
earlyWarningSigns: Puffy, swollen hands and fingers with Raynaud phenomenon in a middle-aged woman — nailfold capillaroscopy showing scleroderma-pattern changes (dilated or drop-out capillaries) should prompt ANA and SSc-specific antibody testing even before skin thickening appears.
Isolated Raynaud phenomenon with abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy and positive ANA — represents early or pre-SSc and warrants annual monitoring.
New-onset GERD or dysphagia in a patient with Raynaud and positive ANA — esophageal dysmotility is an early visceral manifestation.
Unexplained exertional dyspnea with reduced DLCO on PFTs in a known SSc patient — early PAH before symptoms of right heart failure.
emergencySigns: Acute hypertension with rising creatinine in a patient with dcSSc — scleroderma renal crisis, requiring urgent ACE inhibitor therapy (captopril titrated to blood pressure control) to prevent irreversible acute kidney injury and dialysis.
Rapidly progressive dyspnea with hypoxia — acute interstitial pneumonia or right heart failure from PAH, requiring emergency hospital admission.
Digital gangrene with tissue necrosis — requires IV prostaglandin therapy and urgent vascular or hand surgery assessment.
Sudden severe epigastric pain with melena in a patient with gastric antral vascular ectasia (watermelon stomach) — a cause of occult GI bleeding in SSc requiring endoscopic therapy.

causes.cause: Autoantibody-mediated immune activation and fibroblast stimulation
causes.description: SSc-specific autoantibodies (anti-topoisomerase I, anticentromere, anti-RNA polymerase III) correlate with clinical subtypes and may directly stimulate fibroblast collagen production. Anti-RNA polymerase III is associated with both diffuse skin disease and increased scleroderma renal crisis risk.

Structural and functional endothelial cell injury is among the earliest pathological events. Endothelin-1 overproduction, reduced prostacyclin synthesis, and platelet activation cause progressive obliterative microangiopathy in small vessels. The result is chronic tissue hypoxia, which upregulates TGF-β and promotes further fibrosis.

causes.cause: TGF-β pathway dysregulation and myofibroblast activation
causes.description: Transforming growth factor beta is the central mediator of fibrosis in SSc. TGF-β activates fibroblasts to differentiate into myofibroblasts that deposit excessive collagen type I and type III. Multiple upstream drivers — immune complexes, PDGF, hypoxia — converge on this pathway.

SSc has a heritability of approximately 15-20%. Risk alleles include HLA-DQB1*0501 (associated with anticentromere antibody), HLA-DRB1*11 (anti-Scl-70), and IRF5, STAT4, and BLK variants. Concordance in identical twins is only 4.7%, indicating strong environmental co-triggers.

Crystalline silica (miners, sandblasters, stonemasons) increases SSc risk 3-fold in men. Organic solvents, vinyl chloride, and aromatic hydrocarbon exposure have been implicated. Silica activates innate immunity and stimulates TGF-β production in macrophages.

riskFactors.description: Women account for approximately 80% of SSc cases. Estrogen influences immune regulation and fibroblast TGF-β sensitivity. The female predominance is highest during reproductive years.
Disease incidence peaks in the fourth to sixth decade. Onset before age 20 is rare but carries a higher risk of overlap features and more severe organ involvement.
Black women in the US have a 3-fold higher incidence of SSc than White women and are more likely to develop diffuse cutaneous disease, ILD, and cardiac complications at a younger age.
Crystalline silica exposure in mining, quarrying, and construction increases SSc risk 3-fold. IARC classifies crystalline silica as a Group 1 carcinogen; it also triggers systemic autoimmunity.
Aromatic and chlorinated solvents used in dry cleaning, painting, and chemical manufacturing have been associated with elevated SSc risk in case-control and cohort studies.

First-degree relatives of SSc patients have a 13-fold higher rate of SSc than the general population, and elevated rates of SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, and thyroid disease — reflecting shared polygenic susceptibility.

riskFactors.factor: SSc-specific autoantibody positivity without clinical disease
riskFactors.description: Individuals with anti-RNA polymerase III or anti-Scl-70 positivity and abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy have markedly elevated short-term risk of developing full SSc. These represent a pre-clinical at-risk population.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 35-65 constitute the majority; in the US, SSc affects approximately 49,000 women and 12,000 men.
Black women have 3-fold higher incidence than White women and more severe dcSSc phenotype with earlier lung and heart complications.
Choctaw Native Americans in Oklahoma have one of the highest reported SSc prevalence rates in the world (469 per 100,000), with a high frequency of anti-Scl-70 antibody.
Pediatric SSc (onset under 16) represents 2-3% of all SSc; juvenile lcSSc is more common than dcSSc and has a better long-term functional prognosis.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of SSc uses the 2013 ACR/EULAR criteria, which award weighted points for skin thickening of the fingers proximal to the MCPs (sufficient alone), fingertip changes (digital ulcers, pitting scars), telangiectasia, abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy, SSc-specific autoantibodies (anticentromere, anti-Scl-70, anti-RNA polymerase III), pulmonary involvement (ILD or PAH), and Raynaud phenomenon. A total score of 9 or more classifies a patient as SSc with high specificity. The workup at diagnosis includes ANA with titer and pattern (speckled suggests anti-Scl-70; centromere pattern suggests anticentromere antibody), an ENA panel to identify specific antibodies, nailfold capillaroscopy to grade microangiopathy (early, active, or late scleroderma pattern), HRCT chest (for ILD), pulmonary function tests including DLCO, echocardiography (for PAH screening), barium swallow or oesophageal manometry (for dysmotility), ECG and Holter monitoring (for arrhythmias and conduction disease), urinalysis with protein-creatinine ratio, and renal function including creatinine. Repeat 6-monthly echocardiography and annual PFTs are recommended in the first 5 years for dcSSc and the first 10 years for lcSSc given the risk of delayed PAH.

diagnosticTests.test: ANA with pattern and SSc-specific ENA panel (anti-Scl-70, anticentromere, anti-RNA Pol III)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies SSc-specific antibodies that predict subtype and organ complication risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. ANA testing returns within 2-3 days; ENA panel within 5-7 days. Centromere pattern correlates with lcSSc and PAH risk; anti-Scl-70 correlates with dcSSc and ILD; anti-RNA Pol III correlates with dcSSc and renal crisis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades microvascular damage (early, active, late scleroderma pattern); distinguishes SSc Raynaud from primary Raynaud
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A camera lens is applied to the nailfold under immersion oil. Takes 10-15 minutes; non-invasive. Dilated capillaries with drop-out define scleroderma pattern and predict high risk of developing SSc from early undifferentiated CTD.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and characterizes interstitial lung disease — predominant pattern is non-specific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) with ground-glass opacity and reticulation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-minute CT, no contrast required. Results interpreted by a specialist radiologist, usually reported within 24-48 hours. Estimated % lung involvement on HRCT guides treatment thresholds.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies lung restriction and gas transfer impairment from ILD; DLCO reduction may precede CT changes in early PAH
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry and gas transfer measurement in a clinic booth, 30-45 minutes. FVC below 70% predicted and DLCO below 70% define meaningful impairment. Baseline PFTs repeated annually.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for pulmonary arterial hypertension (RVSP above 35 mmHg) and pericardial effusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minute cardiac ultrasound. Elevated RVSP prompts right heart catheterization for definitive PAH diagnosis. Annual echo recommended in both lcSSc and dcSSc.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardized clinical assessment of skin thickening at 17 body sites; primary outcome measure in clinical trials and for monitoring dcSSc progression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination by a trained physician; no procedures required. Score ranges 0-51; a fall of 5 points or more is considered clinically meaningful improvement.

treatmentOverview: SSc treatment is organ-specific because no single agent modifies the underlying fibrotic process across all systems. The 2017 EULAR recommendations for SSc management organize treatment by organ domain. For Raynaud phenomenon and digital ulcers: dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (nifedipine 30-60 mg/day) are first-line; PDE-5 inhibitors (sildenafil 20 mg three times daily) are added or substituted for severe Raynaud; IV iloprost (prostacyclin analogue) is used in digital ulcer crisis. For interstitial lung disease: mycophenolate mofetil (3 g/day) is the current first-line per ACR 2020 guidelines, having demonstrated non-inferiority to cyclophosphamide in the SLS-II trial with better tolerability; nintedanib (150 mg twice daily), an anti-fibrotic tyrosine kinase inhibitor, slows FVC decline in SSc-ILD per the SENSCIS trial and is now combined with mycophenolate in progressive disease. For PAH: combination therapy with an endothelin receptor antagonist (ambrisentan or macitentan) plus a PDE-5 inhibitor (tadalafil) is first-line for most patients per 2022 ESC/ERS guidelines. For scleroderma renal crisis: ACE inhibitors (captopril titrated urgently to blood-pressure target) are the cornerstone; outcome has improved dramatically from near-universal fatal outcome pre-captopril era to 55-70% 5-year survival. For diffuse skin disease: methotrexate reduces mRSS by approximately 40% versus placebo; tocilizumab (anti-IL-6 receptor) received FDA approval for SSc-ILD in 2021 and also reduces skin progression. Autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is considered for progressive refractory dcSSc in specialized centers.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (1.5-3 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line immunosuppressive for SSc-ILD and skin disease. Non-inferior to cyclophosphamide with better GI tolerability in SLS-II trial. Requires regular CBC, LFT, and renal monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilizes FVC in 60-70% of SSc-ILD patients over 2 years; reduces mRSS by 25-35% in dcSSc

medicalTreatments.name: Nintedanib (150 mg twice daily with food)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-fibrotic agent approved for SSc-ILD and progressive fibrosing ILDs. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting PDGF-R, VEGF-R, and FGF-R. Main side-effects are diarrhoea and nausea; liver enzyme monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduced annual FVC decline by 44% (57 mL/year vs 93 mL/year) in SENSCIS trial versus placebo

medicalTreatments.name: Tocilizumab (162 mg subcutaneous weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibody approved for SSc-ILD. Reduces FVC decline and slows skin progression. Requires screening for TB and hepatitis B before initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Slowed FVC decline by approximately 4.2% predicted in focuSSced trial; FDA-approved in 2021 for SSc-ILD

medicalTreatments.name: Nifedipine extended-release (30-60 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for Raynaud phenomenon. Reduces frequency and severity of color-change attacks. Headache and ankle oedema are common side-effects at higher doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Attack frequency reduced by 30-50% versus placebo in pooled calcium-channel blocker trials

medicalTreatments.name: Ambrisentan + tadalafil (combination endothelin/PDE-5 inhibition)
medicalTreatments.description: Guideline-recommended combination for newly diagnosed intermediate- to high-risk PAH. AMBITION trial demonstrated 50% reduction in time to clinical worsening versus monotherapy. Both drugs prescribed by PAH-specialist centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% reduction in clinical worsening events (AMBITION trial, NEJM 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: Captopril (12.5 mg every 8 hours, titrated upward) for scleroderma renal crisis
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory urgent intervention for scleroderma renal crisis (new hypertension >150/85 plus rising creatinine in dcSSc). ACE inhibition blocks the RAAS-driven malignant hypertension of renal crisis. Do not withhold even if creatinine is rising.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Transformed outcome from >80% 1-year mortality to 55-70% 5-year survival; patients may still require short-term dialysis but often recover renal function within 12 months

surgicalOptions.name: Sympathectomy (digital or cervical)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical interruption of the sympathetic nerve supply to reduce vasospasm in severe Raynaud or digital ischemia. Most commonly digital sympathectomy around the palmar arch and common digital arteries.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Ulcer healing in 60-75% and symptom improvement in 70-80% at 6 months in published series; benefit may not be sustained at 24 months

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Single or double lung transplant for end-stage SSc-ILD or PAH unresponsive to all medical therapy. SSc patients are highly selected; GI motility and esophageal function are critical factors in transplant candidacy because aspiration from esophageal dysmotility causes rapid graft loss.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 1-year survival 65-70% in well-selected SSc patients at experienced centers; 5-year survival 40-50%

alternativeTreatments.name: Autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (AHSCT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Immune ablation followed by reconstitution with autologous stem cells. The ASTIS and SCOT trials demonstrated improved event-free survival versus cyclophosphamide in refractory dcSSc at 5 years, at the cost of higher early treatment-related mortality. Reserved for patients under 65 with progressive dcSSc in specialized centers.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with suspected SSc should be evaluated by a rheumatologist with expertise in connective tissue diseases for baseline organ assessment, SSc-specific antibody interpretation, and subtype classification. Multidisciplinary input from pulmonology (ILD, PAH), cardiology (PAH, arrhythmia), gastroenterology (dysmotility, watermelon stomach), and nephrology (renal crisis) is required for comprehensive management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist at a center accredited for SSc management, ideally an EUSTAR (EULAR Scleroderma Trials and Research) network site or a FASC (Fibrosing Alveolar Scleroderma Center) member institution. For PAH, ensure the center has a dedicated PAH service with right heart catheterization capability. Lung transplant centers require specific SSc experience.
hospitalCriteria: Rheumatology service with nailfold capillaroscopy and SSc-specific antibody testing
ILD program with HRCT, PFTs, and bronchoscopy capability
PAH-specialist center with right heart catheterization
Gastroenterology service experienced in SSc esophageal and gastric management
Lung transplant program for end-stage ILD or PAH
keyFacilities: Academic rheumatology centers with stem cell transplant capability
preventionStrategies: Avoid occupational silica and organic solvent exposures; use respiratory protective equipment where mandatory exposure cannot be eliminated.
Protect hands and the whole body from cold exposure with heated gloves, hand warmers, and layered clothing to minimize Raynaud attacks and their ischemic consequences.
Annual influenza and 5-yearly pneumococcal vaccination to prevent respiratory infections that can precipitate ILD exacerbations.
High-SPF photoprotection (SPF 50+) to minimize UV-triggered immune activation.
Aggressive GERD management (high-dose proton pump inhibitors) to prevent aspiration, a cause of ILD exacerbation.
lifestyleModifications: Strict no-smoking policy: smoking accelerates vascular disease, increases PAH risk, and worsens Raynaud phenomenon.
Regular hand physiotherapy and passive range-of-motion exercises to slow contracture formation from sclerodactyly.
Elevate the head of the bed 15-20 cm and avoid lying flat for 2-3 hours after meals to reduce nocturnal GERD and aspiration risk.
Eat small, frequent meals (5-6 per day) rather than large meals to accommodate esophageal dysmotility and reduce reflux.
Oral exercises and moisturizing to maintain oral aperture and prevent perioral fibrosis from worsening.
Engage in aerobic exercise 30 minutes per day as tolerated between exacerbations to preserve cardiovascular fitness and counteract deconditioning.

recommended: Small, frequent meals (5-6 daily) with soft or semi-solid textures to accommodate esophageal dysmotility and reduce aspiration risk
Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to maintain muscle mass during inflammation and possible malnutrition from GI dysmotility
Calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-2,000 IU/day) supplementation for patients on long-term corticosteroids
Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish twice weekly or fish oil supplement) to modulate inflammatory activity
avoid: Large meals, lying flat after eating, and eating within 3 hours of bedtime — all worsen GERD and aspiration risk
Alcohol, which worsens esophageal dysmotility and interacts with methotrexate or mycophenolate metabolism
Very high supplemental calcium doses without vitamin D, which may worsen vascular calcinosis in lcSSc
Raw or undercooked foods in immunosuppressed patients — increased infection risk from SSc-related bowel dysmotility

exerciseGuidelines: Graded aerobic exercise (30 minutes of walking or cycling, 5 days/week) is safe and improves fatigue and functional capacity in stable SSc. Hand physiotherapy reduces contractures. Breathing exercises (diaphragmatic breathing, pursed-lip breathing) and pulmonary rehabilitation benefit patients with ILD. Exercise in cold environments triggers Raynaud attacks and should be avoided or performed with thermal protection.
dailyManagement: Apply moisturizers to skin several times daily to reduce dryness and fissuring; early digital ulcers require wound dressings and topical vasodilators.
Take PPI (omeprazole 20-40 mg twice daily) before meals to control GERD and reduce aspiration risk; prokinetics (domperidone, metoclopramide) may be added for gastroparesis.
Monitor home blood pressure twice weekly in dcSSc patients — a sudden rise above 150/85 mmHg in the first 5 years warrants same-day medical assessment for renal crisis.
Record exertional tolerance weekly with a standardized walk test; report new or worsening dyspnea promptly to your rheumatologist or pulmonologist.
Keep an emergency card documenting the SSc diagnosis, SSc renal crisis protocol (start captopril), and current medications for emergency physician awareness.
prognosis: Prognosis varies substantially by subtype. Limited cutaneous SSc has a 10-year survival of 75-85% in modern cohorts; most deaths result from PAH occurring late in the disease course. Diffuse cutaneous SSc has a more serious short-term prognosis: 10-year survival is 55-65% in older cohorts, though this has improved to 70-80% in centers with early ILD and renal crisis intervention. Scleroderma renal crisis, previously almost universally fatal, now has a 55-70% 5-year survival with ACE inhibitor therapy, though some patients require long-term dialysis. SSc-ILD stabilizes or improves in over 60% of patients treated with mycophenolate and nintedanib; progressive ILD to respiratory failure occurs in 20-30%. PAH with SSc has a worse prognosis than idiopathic PAH despite the same drug therapy; 5-year survival with PAH-specific combination therapy is approximately 50-60%. Pregnancy in SSc requires careful planning; scleroderma renal crisis risk is increased in the third trimester, and perinatal loss rates are higher than background.
recoveryTimeline: Raynaud attacks: improved within days of calcium-channel blocker initiation. Skin: mRSS improvements with mycophenolate or tocilizumab visible at 6-12 months. ILD: FVC stabilization within 12 months of mycophenolate; nintedanib benefit accumulates over 1-2 years. PAH: functional class improvement within 3-6 months of combination therapy. Renal crisis: BP control within days; renal function recovery over 6-12 months if captopril is started promptly.
complications: Scleroderma renal crisis — new hypertension with rapidly rising creatinine in dcSSc; requires same-day treatment with captopril; annual home BP monitoring in first 5 years of dcSSc.
Progressive ILD leading to respiratory failure — the most common organ-related cause of death; monitor FVC annually and initiate nintedanib when progression is confirmed.
Pulmonary arterial hypertension — develops in 10-20% of lcSSc and 5-10% of dcSSc; annual echocardiography for early detection when treatment response is best.
Gastrointestinal dysmotility from esophageal to colonic involvement causing malnutrition, bacterial overgrowth, and fecal incontinence in advanced disease.
Digital gangrene and amputation from severe obliterative vasculopathy — digital sympathectomy or IV prostacyclin can salvage digits in crisis.
Cardiac complications including ventricular arrhythmias, conduction system disease, and restrictive cardiomyopathy from myocardial fibrosis — Holter monitoring and echocardiography annually in dcSSc.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy and support organization with helpline, patient education, and research funding for systemic sclerosis.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma Society UK (SRUK)
supportResources.description: UK patient organization providing information, peer support, and nurse advice line for SSc patients.

supportResources.name: EULAR Scleroderma Trials and Research (EUSTAR) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: European network with patient and clinician resources on SSc management and clinical trials.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MCTD features puffy fingers, Raynaud, and anti-U1 RNP antibody with overlapping lupus and myositis features, but lacks the progressive skin fibrosis and the skin-restricted subtypes of SSc. Anti-Scl-70 and anticentromere antibodies are negative in classic MCTD. About 15-25% of MCTD patients eventually develop SSc-predominant disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Raynaud Phenomenon
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary Raynaud occurs in young healthy individuals with no underlying disease, normal nailfold capillaroscopy, and negative ANA. SSc-associated Raynaud shows abnormal (scleroderma-pattern) capillaroscopy, positive SSc-specific antibodies, and progresses to digital ulcers and skin changes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dermatomyositis produces heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, and proximal myopathy with myositis-specific antibodies. SSc skin changes are fibrotic thickening rather than inflammatory rash. Both can cause ILD but differ in distribution, antibody profile, and skin examination findings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLE features malar rash, oral ulcers, serositis, nephritis, and anti-dsDNA antibody. Skin involvement in SLE is inflammatory rather than fibrotic. SSc skin changes are palpable hard thickening (sclerodactyly, induration) rather than the photosensitive erythema of SLE.

relatedConditions.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Major overlap syndrome with SSc sharing Raynaud, ILD, PAH, and esophageal dysmotility; distinguished by anti-U1 RNP positivity and absence of true skin fibrosis in most MCTD cases.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major systemic CTD managed by the same rheumatology team; SSc-SLE overlap occurs in about 5% of SSc patients.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory myopathy that can co-occur with SSc in overlap syndrome; both conditions can produce ILD.

relatedConditions.name: Raynaud Phenomenon
relatedConditions.relevance: Present in over 95% of SSc patients and is the most common first symptom; distinguishing secondary (SSc-related) from primary Raynaud is the critical early step.

faqs.question: What is scleroderma?
faqs.answer: Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) is a chronic autoimmune disease causing progressive fibrosis (hardening) of the skin and internal organs, combined with blood vessel damage and immune activation. It affects about 20-50 per 100,000 people, predominantly women aged 35-65. Raynaud phenomenon is almost always the first symptom.

faqs.question: What is the difference between limited and diffuse scleroderma?
faqs.answer: Limited cutaneous SSc (formerly CREST) confines skin thickening to the hands, face, and lower arms, with late PAH risk and anticentromere antibody. Diffuse cutaneous SSc spreads rapidly to the trunk and proximal limbs within 5 years, with early ILD, scleroderma renal crisis, and anti-Scl-70 antibody. Diffuse disease carries a more serious short-term prognosis.

faqs.question: What causes scleroderma?
faqs.answer: Systemic sclerosis results from immune dysregulation producing autoantibodies, endothelial cell injury causing vascular damage, and TGF-beta pathway overactivation driving fibrosis. Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DQB1, IRF5, STAT4) combined with environmental triggers such as silica exposure, viral infection, or organic solvents precipitates disease in predisposed individuals.

faqs.question: Is scleroderma curable?
faqs.answer: Scleroderma has no cure. No therapy reverses established fibrosis. However, targeted treatment of lung disease (mycophenolate, nintedanib), pulmonary hypertension (ambrisentan, sildenafil), and renal crisis (captopril) substantially improves survival and quality of life. Early diagnosis and organ-specific monitoring are the most important prognostic factors.

faqs.question: What antibody is positive in scleroderma?
faqs.answer: Anticentromere antibody is positive in 60-70% of limited cutaneous SSc and predicts PAH risk. Anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70) is positive in 25-35% of diffuse cutaneous SSc and predicts ILD. Anti-RNA polymerase III is associated with diffuse SSc and scleroderma renal crisis. ANA is positive in over 95% of all SSc patients.

faqs.question: How serious is scleroderma?
faqs.answer: Limited cutaneous SSc has a 10-year survival of 75-85%. Diffuse cutaneous SSc has a more serious course with 55-70% 10-year survival in older cohorts, improving to 70-80% in modern series. Pulmonary arterial hypertension and interstitial lung disease are the main killers. Early detection of organ complications drives the best outcomes.

faqs.question: What is scleroderma renal crisis?
faqs.answer: Scleroderma renal crisis is an emergency complication of diffuse SSc: sudden severe hypertension (above 150/85) with rapidly rising creatinine from renal artery spasm and fibrinoid necrosis. It requires same-day ACE inhibitor therapy (captopril). Before captopril, it was nearly universally fatal; now 55-70% of patients survive 5 years.

faqs.question: Does scleroderma cause lung problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Interstitial lung disease occurs in 35-75% of SSc patients and is the leading cause of SSc-related death. Pulmonary arterial hypertension develops in 10-20% of limited SSc and 5-10% of diffuse SSc. Annual pulmonary function tests and echocardiography are recommended for early detection.

faqs.question: How is scleroderma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses 2013 ACR/EULAR criteria: skin thickening proximal to the MCPs, SSc-specific antibodies (anti-Scl-70, anticentromere, anti-RNA Pol III), abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy, Raynaud phenomenon, and organ involvement. Baseline workup includes HRCT chest, echo, PFTs, and renal function.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for scleroderma skin?
faqs.answer: Methotrexate reduces skin thickness (mRSS) by approximately 40% versus placebo in diffuse SSc. Mycophenolate mofetil and tocilizumab also reduce skin progression. No therapy fully reverses established fibrosis; early treatment during the inflammatory phase yields the best outcomes.

faqs.question: Can scleroderma affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cardiac fibrosis causes ventricular arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities, and restrictive cardiomyopathy in 10-20% of dcSSc patients. Annual ECG, Holter monitoring, and echocardiography are recommended. Cardiac MRI detects myocardial fibrosis before symptoms develop.

faqs.question: Is scleroderma hereditary?
faqs.answer: SSc has a genetic component (heritability ~15-20%) but is not directly inherited. First-degree relatives have a 13-fold higher risk of SSc than the general population. Specific HLA alleles and immune gene variants (IRF5, STAT4) increase susceptibility, but a strong environmental trigger is also required.

faqs.question: What is CREST syndrome?
faqs.answer: CREST syndrome is the historical name for limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis: Calcinosis, Raynaud phenomenon, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly (tight finger skin), and Telangiectasia. It is now classified as limited cutaneous SSc (lcSSc) within the 2013 ACR/EULAR scleroderma criteria.

faqs.question: How does scleroderma affect the esophagus?
faqs.answer: Smooth muscle fibrosis in the lower two-thirds of the esophagus causes dysmotility, gastroesophageal reflux, dysphagia for solids, and risk of aspiration. Over 75% of SSc patients are affected. Treatment includes high-dose PPIs, prokinetics, small frequent meals, and head-of-bed elevation.

faqs.question: Can you have scleroderma without skin thickening?
faqs.answer: Yes. SSc sine scleroderma describes internal organ involvement with SSc-specific autoantibodies and Raynaud phenomenon but without significant skin thickening. It accounts for 2-5% of SSc diagnoses and is identified by positive SSc antibodies combined with ILD, PAH, or esophageal disease.

faqs.question: Does diet affect scleroderma?
faqs.answer: No specific diet treats SSc, but dietary adjustments help manage symptoms. Small, frequent meals reduce GERD and aspiration from esophageal dysmotility. Calcium and vitamin D support bone health in patients on corticosteroids. Omega-3 fatty acids may reduce systemic inflammation.

metaDescription: Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis): autoimmune fibrosis of skin and organs. Subtypes, Raynaud, lung disease, and renal crisis reviewed by aihealz.
